text.title: pytest Test Patterns
text.summary: AAA structure, parametrize (basic/IDs/cartesian/indirect), mocking with unittest.mock and pytest-mock, async tests, and debugging.

section.title: Arrange-Act-Assert
section.rule: Every test follows Arrange → Act → Assert with a blank line between sections.

section.title: Parametrize
section.rule: Use @pytest.mark.parametrize to test multiple cases without code duplication.
Always provide IDs for parametrized cases to make failure messages readable.
Use indirect parametrize to pass parameters through fixtures when fixture setup depends on the parameter.

section.title: Mocking with pytest-mock
section.rule: Use mocker.patch() in test body (not conftest) for per-test mocks.
Use mocker.patch.object() when patching a method on a specific instance or class.
Always assert calls on mocks — silent mocks that never fire are useless.

antipattern.description: Patching at import location instead of usage location
antipattern.code:
# BAD: patches the original module, not where it's used mocker.patch("myapp.utils.requests.get")
# GOOD: patch where it's imported/used mocker.patch("myapp.services.payment.requests.get")
antipattern.reason: Python looks up names at call time in the namespace where they were imported, not the original module.

section.title: Async Tests
section.rule: Set asyncio_mode = "auto" in pyproject.toml to avoid decorating every async test.
Use pytest-anyio or pytest-asyncio — never run event loops manually in tests.

example.code: # asyncio_mode = "auto" in pyproject.toml async def test_fetch_user(async_client): response = await async_client.get("/api/users/1") assert response.status_code == 200 assert response.json()["id"] == 1
